topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on my kitchen counter, an incongruous splash of vibrant color against the sterile white granite. I hadn't ordered anything. I certainly hadn't expected anything. My gaze drifted to the arrangement inside—a shocking cascade of deep crimson roses, their petals unnaturally perfect, interspersed with stark, almost black lilies. There was no note. Just the flowers, sitting there like silent, accusatory witnesses. My hands, usually steady, began to tremble as I reached for the box. The paper felt strangely cold beneath my fingertips. I pulled the lid open, and the scent—too rich, too heavy, like something preserved in amber—hit me immediately. I backed away slowly, my breath catching in my throat, my eyes darting around the empty room as if searching for an unseen threat lurking just beyond the periphery of my vision.

[story 2]

It was Tuesday afternoon, and I was attempting to focus on a particularly tedious spreadsheet when the delivery arrived. A plain brown package, addressed simply to my name. Inside, nestled in tissue paper that seemed to absorb the light, were a handful of pale, almost phosphorescent blue forget-me-nots. No inscription. No signature. My fingers, clumsy with sudden anxiety, unfolded the wrapping. The sheer, unadorned presentation was deeply unsettling. I stared at the blossoms, a knot tightening in my chest, a sudden, sharp awareness that something was profoundly wrong with this simple gesture. I dropped the box onto the floor, the delicate petals scattering across the hardwood, and I couldn't bring myself to touch them again.

[story 3]

When the doorbell rang, I braced myself for a neighbor or a misplaced package. Instead, there it was: a lavish bouquet of sunflowers, impossibly bright, their heads turned towards me with an almost predatory intensity. The wrapping paper was thick, expensive, and utterly bare. My jaw went slack. I stood motionless by the entryway, my knuckles white where I gripped the doorframe. Every instinct screamed at me to recoil, to slam the door shut and pretend this spectacle never occurred. I took a shallow, ragged breath, trying to regulate the sudden, frantic pounding beneath my ribs, while my eyes remained locked on the unsolicited, silent offering.
